name: Park, Woojin
recent_1: Ryu, J., Moon, P., Hwang, K., Yoon, S., Lee, Y., Piao, Q., Park, J.-H., & Park, W. (2026). Comparing Paper- and AR-Based Assembly Manuals in Task Performance, dlPFC Hemodynamic Responses, and Perceived Workload. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261439486
recent_1_synopsis: An experiment compared paper-based and augmented-reality (AR) assembly manuals, measuring task performance, dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC) hemodynamic responses via fNIRS, and perceived workload. The study links instruction-medium design to neurophysiological indices of cognitive load, advancing evidence-based guidance for AR work-instruction systems in manufacturing and assembly contexts.
recent_2: Kim, S., Hwang, K., Park, W., & et al. (2024). Effects of Armor Design and Marksmanship Posture on Performance, Postural Sway and Perceived Workload During a Military Rifle Marksmanship Task. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241276267
recent_2_synopsis: This study examined how body-armor design and marksmanship posture jointly affect shooting performance, postural sway, and perceived workload in a military rifle task. It connects occupational biomechanics and equipment ergonomics to operational performance, informing protective-equipment and training design for soldiers.
recent_3: Ban, G., & Park, W. (2024). Effects of In-Vehicle Touchscreen Location on Driver Task Performance, Eye Gaze Behavior, and Workload During Conditionally Automated Driving: Nondriving-Related Task and Take-Over. Human Factors, 66(12), 2651-2668. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241226838
recent_3_synopsis: Using an SAE Level 3 conditionally automated driving simulator, this study evaluated how in-vehicle touchscreen placement influences nondriving-related task performance, eye-gaze behavior, workload, and take-over quality. The findings inform interior layout and HMI design for automated vehicles where drivers alternate between secondary tasks and resuming control.
seminal_1: Beck, D., Jung, J., & Park, W. (2019). Evaluating the Effects of In-Vehicle Side-View Display Layout Design on Physical Demands of Driving. Human Factors, 63(2), 348-363. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819884781
seminal_1_synopsis: This study quantified how the layout of in-vehicle side-view (camera monitor system) displays affects the physical/postural demands of driving, integrating vehicle ergonomics with occupational biomechanics. With roughly 19 citations (Crossref, June 2026), it is among Park's most-cited Human Factors articles and is foundational to his program on biomechanically grounded in-vehicle display design.
seminal_2: Ban, G., & Park, W. (2024). Effects of In-Vehicle Touchscreen Location on Driver Task Performance, Eye Gaze Behavior, and Workload During Conditionally Automated Driving: Nondriving-Related Task and Take-Over. Human Factors, 66(12), 2651-2668. DOI: 10.1177/00187208241226838
seminal_2_synopsis: This recent Human Factors paper on touchscreen placement during conditionally automated driving has accumulated roughly 14 citations (Crossref, June 2026) within about two years, an unusually fast accrual that makes it one of Park's most influential works. It is foundational for current automated-driving HMI research linking secondary-task interface location to take-over performance and workload.
expertise_overview: Park's research centers on occupational biomechanics and cognitive (physical) ergonomics, with strong emphasis on vehicle and in-vehicle interface ergonomics. Recurring topics include driver task performance, workload, and eye-gaze behavior during conditionally automated driving; in-vehicle display and touchscreen layout; postural demands, postural sway, and load carriage; and protective-equipment design. Methodologically he combines controlled human-subjects experiments and driving/task simulators with biomechanical measurement (posture, sway, muscular strength, reachable-workspace modeling), perceived-workload assessment, and increasingly neurophysiological measures such as fNIRS hemodynamic responses of the prefrontal cortex. He also works on human-reachable-workspace and discomfort-prediction models, smart-chair posture sensing, and usability of interactive systems. His application domains span automotive HMI and automated driving, manufacturing/assembly with augmented reality, and military and occupational task performance.
notes: Confidently identified as Woojin Park, Department of Industrial Engineering, Seoul National University (Life Enhancing Technology Lab; PhD Industrial & Operations Engineering, University of Michigan, 2003). Identity confirmed by the four pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers (all resolved via Crossref with 'Woojin Park' as author/co-author, June 2026) plus ResearchGate profile (Woojin-Park-6), SNU Elsevier Pure, and his lab's site (sites.google.com/view/ergonomicslab, lists Donghyun Beck — co-author on the 2019 side-view paper — as a PhD advisee). Distinguished from several name-collision homonyms: Jaehyun Park (VRSQ, medical-device usability), Jungkun Park (Hanyang, m-payment), Adrian E. Park (surgical ergonomics), and various biomechanics/materials 'Park' authors who surfaced in broad Crossref author queries but are NOT this researcher. All five cited DOIs verified to resolve on Crossref with matching titles/authors. Citation counts are Crossref 'is-referenced-by-count' as of 2026-06-17: AR assembly manual = 0; rifle marksmanship (10.1177/00187208241276267) = 2; touchscreen location = 14; side-view display = 19. No DOIs were invented. Could NOT verify a DOI for two additional recent works seen in web search (a 2025 Applied Ergonomics paper on population-accommodation evaluation for adjustable products, and a 2023 International Journal of Industrial Ergonomics rifle/load-mass paper); these were therefore excluded from the cited entries. The recent_3 / touchscreen paper is used as seminal_2 because it is among the most-cited verified works; it intentionally overlaps with recent_3.
